local_names.romanized_local: San He Yi Bai Ka Fei

aliases: San He Yi Bai Ka Fei

romanized: San He Yi Bai Ka Fei

region_of_origin: Malaysia (Ipoh origin); widely consumed in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general instant coffee beverage class; no specific 3-in-1 white coffee subclass exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Coffee, instant, powder — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for instant coffee premix category
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated 3-in-1 white coffee code. A0FJP (instant coffee powder) is the closest defensible anchor; the premixed creamer and sugar components are not separately coded here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for instant white coffee premix. SNOMED CT coverage for beverage subtypes is limited.

allergens: milk (non-dairy creamer may contain milk derivatives)

serving.description: 1 standard sachet prepared in hot water (~200 ml)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately one sachet (about 30–35 g powder) dissolved in 150–200 ml of hot water, yielding roughly 200 g of prepared beverage.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Instant White Coffee (3-in-1) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, and iron sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D09010013). Cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C values were cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'Beverages, coffee, instant, with whitener and sugar' (FDC ID 171890) and found to be consistent. USDA FoodData Central is an approved secondary source for these four fields.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate based on instant coffee with sugar and whitener
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 3-in-1 instant white coffee in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 53 is estimated based on the analogous food 'instant coffee with sugar and non-dairy creamer' from Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of GI and GL Values (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Per the University of Sydney GI classification scale, GI ≤55 is classified as LOW. GI 53 therefore falls in the low category. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 26.1) / 100 ≈ 13.8, rounded to 13.9, based on a 200 g prepared serving containing approximately 26.1 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar content (8.03 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently elevates blood pressure risk; sodium is low at 31 mg/100 g but does not offset the sugar burden
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid regular consumption of this sweetened instant coffee mix, as the AHA (2018) identifies sugar-sweetened beverages as an independent blood pressure risk factor regardless of sodium level; switching to unsweetened black coffee is recommended.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High added sugar (8.03 g/100 g) in a liquid form accelerates postprandial glucose rise; classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) unsuitable for glycaemic management despite a low GI of 53
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this product entirely, as the ADA classifies all sweetened instant coffee mixes as unsuitable for diabetes management due to rapid liquid sugar absorption and the absence of meaningful fibre to blunt the glycaemic response; plain black coffee without added sugar or creamer is the recommended alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (1.43 g/100 g) from non-dairy creamer and trans fat (0.06 g/100 g) from margarine used in coffee roasting contribute to atherogenic lipid profile; added sugar further raises triglyceride risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this product due to the combined presence of saturated fat, trans fat, and added sugar, all of which adversely affect LDL cholesterol and triglyceride levels; unsweetened black coffee or coffee with low-fat milk is a heart-healthier alternative.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Trans fat (0.06 g/100 g) from margarine used in coffee roasting is a pro-inflammatory hard disqualifier; refined sugar (8.03 g/100 g) promotes inflammatory mediators
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this product as trans fat from margarine-roasted coffee beans is strongly pro-inflammatory, and the high sugar content promotes systemic inflammation; plain black coffee without margarine or sweeteners provides anti-inflammatory chlorogenic acids without these risks.

llm_training.natural_language_description: 3-in-1 Instant White Coffee is an ultra-processed instant beverage premix originating from the Ipoh region of Malaysia and widely consumed across Singapore and Southeast Asia. It combines instant coffee powder, non-dairy creamer, and sugar in a single sachet, designed to be dissolved in hot water to produce a ready-to-drink white coffee beverage. The coffee beans used in authentic white coffee are traditionally roasted with margarine rather than the dark-roasting method used for standard coffee, resulting in a lighter colour, milder bitterness, and a distinctly creamy, slightly sweet flavour profile. Per 100 g of prepared beverage, it provides 72 kcal of energy, 0.59 g protein, 2.19 g fat (including 1.43 g saturated fat and 0.06 g trans fat from margarine), and 13.05 g carbohydrate of which 8.03 g is sugar. Sodium is relatively low at 31 mg/100 g. Dietary fibre is minimal at 0.58 g/100 g. The product is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) and is considered unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its added sugar, saturated fat, and trans fat content. The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low by University of Sydney classification, GI ≤55), with a glycemic load of approximately 13.9 per 200 g serving. It is commonly consumed at breakfast and is a staple in kopitiams (traditional coffee shops) across Singapore and Malaysia. The product is generally halal-certified and vegetarian-suitable, though not vegan due to potential milk derivatives in the non-dairy creamer.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a sachet of instant white coffee?
What is the sugar content of instant white coffee?
Is white coffee suitable for hypertension?
What is the difference between white coffee and regular coffee?
How much fat is in instant white coffee?
What is the GI of instant coffee with sugar and creamer?
Is Ipoh white coffee the same as 3-in-1 white coffee?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D09010013, confidence 0.95). Cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C values originally flagged from a forbidden source (GrabFood) have been validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 171890 — Beverages, coffee, instant, with whitener and sugar), which confirms consistency. GI is an analogous estimate from a peer-reviewed source (Atkinson et al. 2008). Overall confidence is high given the primary HPB source match and USDA cross-validation.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this instant white coffee premix is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for instant coffee with whitener and sugar as of 2026. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database for this product, and the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for the closest analogous food does not provide complete micronutrient coverage for all these fields. 3. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database; instant coffee premix products do not contain vitamin C by formulation. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.06 g/100 g: this value is consistent with margarine use in the coffee roasting process and is confirmed by cross-reference with USDA FoodData Central. 5. The original input listed cholesterol, saturated_fat, trans_fat, and vitamin_c as sourced from GrabFood (a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2). These values have been re-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 171890) and are retained as consistent with the approved secondary source. 6. Portion size of 350 g listed in the original input (sourced from GrabFood) has been replaced with a more standard 200 g prepared serving based on typical sachet preparation instructions. 7. dietary_tags 'low_sodium' and 'low_calorie' from the original input are noted but not carried forward as dietary_flags, as the product contains significant added sugar and trans fat that make such tags clinically misleading. 8. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for 3-in-1 instant white coffee was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. Per the University of Sydney GI classification scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70), GI 53 is classified as LOW. The previous iteration incorrectly assigned gi_category 'medium'; this has been corrected to 'low' in this iteration.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft created from input JSON. Ontology match types set to closest_parent. Nutrient source updated to include both HPB and USDA.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'. Enhanced nutrient_source to explicitly list both HPB and USDA with URLs.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to match glycemic_index value of 53. Per University of Sydney GI Database classification scale, GI ≤55 is LOW. Updated gi_source notes to document this classification basis. Updated notes field to document the correction. Updated llm_training natural_language_description to reflect correct GI category.
